title: The Big Picture: China's customs authority to "support" coal imports
section_path: The Big Picture: China's customs authority to "support" coal imports
section_path_text: The Big Picture: China's customs authority to "support" coal imports
summary:
The Big Picture: China's customs authority to "support" coal imports
A statement from China's customs authority on 31 October outlined "support" for imports of dry bulk commodities such as coal, grain and iron ore-suggesting the freeze on the coal import levy could be extended into next year. This marks an apparent break with (unconfirmed) market reports from July suggesting a coal import target of 400m tonnes for 2023; some 348m tonnes were already imported in January-September. However, Mongolian overland export capacity for (mainly coking) coal into China could expand further thanks to new rail links and cap seaborne coking coal import growth.

children.title: The Big Picture: China's customs authority to "support" coal imports
section_path: The Big Picture: China's customs authority to "support" coal imports
children.section_path_text: The Big Picture: China's customs authority to "support" coal imports
children.summary: A statement from China's customs authority on 31 October outlined "support" for imports of dry bulk commodities such as coal, grain and iron ore-suggesting the freeze on the coal import levy could be extended into next year. This marks an apparent break with (unconfirmed) market reports from July suggesting a coal import target of 400m tonnes for 2023; some 348m tonnes were already imported in January-September.

children.title: Current picture
children.summary: McCloskey reports the statement has been interpreted by some in the industry as a signal of an extension to the freeze on China's coal import levy beyond its current expiry date of 31 December into next year. The freeze was introduced in May 2022.

children.title: Which vessel sizes has China's coal import surge benefitted most?
section_path: Which vessel sizes has China's coal import surge benefitted most?
children.section_path_text: Which vessel sizes has China's coal import surge benefitted most?
children.summary: With year-on-year gains in Chinese coal trades driven by coal from Australia (after a long period of avoidance), Indonesia and Russia (more noticeable after the start of war in Ukraine), the chart on page 2 reveals which vessel sizes have benefitted according to each trade. The return of Australian coal shipments is responsible for the clear majority of the Capesize gains, with shipments from Colombia, Russia (ex-Pacific and from terminals in the west of the country) plus the US making up most of the rest.

children.title: Long term
children.summary: The IEA's report outlines two scenarios for China's coal demand, both of which peak around 2025. Under the Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS), demand declines to below 50% of the peak by 2050 at an average annual rate of 2.3%; whereas under the Announced Pledges Scenario (APS), the decline is far steeper.
